verdict_note: CFR states ToTok 'was revealed to be a mass surveillance tool used by the Emirati government' and lists Government of the UAE as the affiliated actor.
grounding: CFR names the UAE government as operator and 'civil society' (millions of users in the UAE and globally) as the target; the surveillance ran via the ToTok messaging app, so no compromise-style initial vector is described.

verdict_note: CFR states 'The United Arab Emirates, using hackers contracted through CyberPoint, intercepted emails between former First Lady Michelle Obama and Sheikha Moza bint Nasser of Qatar.'
grounding: CFR attributes the interception to the UAE (via CyberPoint contractors) targeting Qatar's royal family communications; the source describes interception of emails but not the specific initial access method, so vector is null.

verdict_note: EIC/Mediapart investigation states Alp Services 'has been contracted by the UAE government to spy on citizens of 18 countries in Europe and beyond'; the UAE government is named as financier/director.
grounding: EIC attributes the surveillance-and-smear operation to the UAE government (via front Ariaf, contractor Alp Services) targeting 1,000+ individuals and 400 organizations across 18 European countries labelled as Muslim Brotherhood affiliates; this is a HUMINT/private-intelligence collection effort, not a network intrusion, so initial_vector is null.

verdict_note: CFR states the Pegasus malware was installed 'at the request of the United Arab Emirates'; target was Hanan Elatr.
grounding: CFR attributes the NSO Pegasus targeting of Khashoggi's widow Hanan Elatr to a UAE request; CFR says the malware was 'allegedly installed while Elatr was detained at the Dubai airport', i.e. physical device access, hence initial_vector=physical.

verdict_note: Talos assesses 'with high confidence that these actions were performed by a state-sponsored actor' but does NOT name China or the MSS; UAT4356/STORM-1849 is not linked to the PRC in this source.
summary: Cisco Talos disclosed ArcaneDoor, an espionage campaign against perimeter network devices that compromised Cisco ASA firewalls at a small set of customers including government networks. The actors, tracked as UAT4356 (Talos) and STORM-1849 (Microsoft), deployed two custom implants: Line Dancer, an in-memory backdoor for command execution and traffic capture, and Line Runner, a persistence mechanism surviving reboots and upgrades. Two ASA vulnerabilities (CVE-2024-20353 and CVE-2024-20359) were used post-compromise; Talos stated it had not determined the initial access vector. Infrastructure was active from late 2023 with peak activity December 2023 to early January 2024. Talos assessed with high confidence that a state-sponsored actor was responsible but did not attribute the campaign to a specific nation.
grounding: Talos lists government networks globally plus telecom and energy organizations as victims (no countries disclosed); initial_vector is null because Talos explicitly states the initial access vector was not determined. Attribution is only 'state-sponsored', so the claimed cn/mss actor ID is not source-supported.

verdict_note: Proofpoint describes 'Chinese state-sponsored threat actors' running the campaigns and names the three component clusters UNK_FistBump, UNK_DropPitch and UNK_SparkyCarp; matches the China-aligned Taiwan-semiconductor cluster actor.
grounding: Proofpoint attributes the activity to China-aligned/Chinese state-sponsored actors targeting Taiwan's semiconductor industry (manufacturing, design, testing) plus financial analysts covering the sector; the initial vector is phishing (employment, investment-collaboration and account-security lures), hence phishing + tw semiconductor primary and finance collateral.

verdict_note: Unit 42 assesses 'with moderate-high confidence that this activity originates in China' and tracks it as CL-STA-0048; matches the claimed China-nexus cluster (MSS not named specifically).
summary: Unit 42 disclosed CL-STA-0048, an espionage campaign against South Asian entities including a telecommunications organization and government employees. The threat actor gained access by exploiting unpatched internet-facing services (IIS, Apache Tomcat and MSSQL servers) and then deployed PlugX backdoors and Cobalt Strike, using a novel 'Hex Staging' technique to deliver payloads. Objectives included stealing personal information of government employees and other sensitive data, with DNS-based exfiltration and database credential theft observed. Unit 42 noted infrastructure and tooling overlaps with the China-nexus DragonRank group and assessed with moderate-high confidence that the activity originates in China.
grounding: Unit 42 names a South Asian telecom organization and government employees as victims (specific country not stated, so country=null); initial access was exploitation of unpatched/known internet-facing services (IIS/Tomcat/MSSQL), i.e. n-day exploitation.

verdict_note: Volexity explicitly equates StormBamboo with 'Evasive Panda' (previously tracked by Volexity as StormCloud); matches the claimed evasive-panda actor.
grounding: Volexity attributes the ISP compromise and DNS poisoning of insecure software-update mechanisms (delivering MACMA and POCOSTICK/MGBot, resolving to a Hong Kong host) to StormBamboo = Evasive Panda; the poisoning of software updates is a supply-chain initial vector. Volexity describes victims only generically as 'victim organizations' with no sector or country, so targets is empty.

verdict_note: ESET attributes CloudScout to the China-aligned group Evasive Panda (aka Daggerfly/StormBamboo/BRONZE HIGHLAND); matches the claimed evasive-panda actor.
summary: ESET researchers discovered CloudScout, a post-compromise .NET toolset used by the China-aligned group Evasive Panda to steal data from cloud services including Google Drive, Gmail and Outlook. CloudScout works by harvesting authenticated web session cookies from compromised hosts and replaying them to access cloud-stored data, bypassing two-factor authentication. The toolset operates as an extension to Evasive Panda's MgBot backdoor, with HTML parsers to extract and exfiltrate targeted information. ESET observed CloudScout used against a government entity and a religious organization in Taiwan between 2022 and 2023.
grounding: ESET names a Taiwanese government entity and a religious organization as the victims of CloudScout (2022-2023); the report does not state how initial access was obtained (CloudScout is a post-compromise toolset), so initial_vector is null.

verdict_note: ESET assesses 'with high confidence that Operation FishMedley was conducted by the FishMonger APT group' and identifies FishMonger as operated by I-SOON; matches the claimed fishmonger actor.
summary: ESET detailed Operation FishMedley, a 2022 global espionage campaign by the FishMonger APT group, which ESET independently assessed is operated by the Chinese contractor I-SOON (corroborated by the US DOJ's March 2025 indictment of I-SOON employees). Across January to October 2022 the operators compromised seven high-profile organizations including governments, NGOs, Catholic/religious organizations and geopolitical think tanks in Taiwan, Hungary, Turkey, Thailand, the United States and France. They deployed modular backdoors such as ShadowPad, SodaMaster, Spyder and RPipeCommander alongside Cobalt Strike and custom credential-stealing and reconnaissance utilities. ESET was unable to identify the initial compromise vectors.
grounding: ESET names governments, NGOs and geopolitical think tanks (plus Catholic organizations) across six countries as victims; because the targeting spans many countries with no single primary, country is null. ESET states it could not identify the initial compromise vectors, so initial_vector is null. Operators_named left empty because the indictment named I-SOON employees, but ESET's report does not list those individuals.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike tracks GENESIS PANDA as a 'China-nexus' adversary acting as an initial access broker; matches the claimed genesis-panda actor (MSS not named specifically).
summary: CrowdStrike's 2025 Threat Hunting Report describes GENESIS PANDA, a China-nexus, cloud-conscious adversary active since at least January 2024 and assessed to act as an initial access broker. The group ran high-volume operations against the financial services, media, telecommunications and technology sectors across at least 11 countries. In one early-March-2025 intrusion CrowdStrike observed GENESIS PANDA exploit a public-facing Jenkins server, then query the cloud instance metadata service (IMDS) to obtain cloud-provider credentials, after which it added SSH keys and created a backdoor access key to maintain and regain access. CrowdStrike assesses its likely objective is access development to enable future intelligence collection.
grounding: CrowdStrike names finance, media, telecom and technology across 11 countries as targets (no specific country, so country=null); initial access in the documented intrusion was exploitation of a public-facing Jenkins server, hence n-day. China-nexus is stated but the MSS is not named, so confidence is strongly_inferred rather than attested at MSS level.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike tracks GLACIAL PANDA as a 'China-nexus' adversary embedded in telecom networks; matches the claimed glacial-panda actor (MSS not named specifically).
summary: CrowdStrike's 2025 Threat Hunting Report describes GLACIAL PANDA, a China-nexus adversary that since at least 2018 has developed and maintained access to telecommunications provider networks to obtain call detail records (CDRs) and related communications telemetry. The group primarily targets telcos' Linux systems and conducts patient, long-term, espionage-driven intrusions, contributing to a 130% rise in nation-state activity in the telecom sector. GLACIAL PANDA deploys trojanized OpenSSH components collectively codenamed ShieldSlide; the trojanized SSH server binary grants backdoor access by authenticating any account, including root, when a hardcoded password is supplied, while harvesting user authentication sessions and credentials. Its objective is assessed to be intelligence collection from telecom infrastructure.
grounding: CrowdStrike identifies the telecommunications sector (carrier networks worldwide) as GLACIAL PANDA's target with no specific country, so country=null; the report focuses on the ShieldSlide trojanized-OpenSSH persistence/credential-harvesting tooling rather than the initial access method, so initial_vector is null.

verdict_note: ANSSI names the intrusion set 'Houken' and states it 'suspects that the Houken intrusion set is operated by the same threat actor as the intrusion set previously described by MANDIANT as UNC5174'; assessed as Chinese-speaking, UTC+8 access broker.
grounding: ANSSI/CERT-FR (CERTFR-2025-CTI-009) documents Sept-Nov 2024 exploitation of three Ivanti CSA zero-days (CVE-2024-8190/-8963/-9380) for initial access to French governmental, telecom, media, finance and transport orgs; a rootkit was deployed on an internet-facing CSA at a French defense-sector entity.

verdict_note: SecurityScorecard STRIKE names the network 'LapDogs' and assesses it 'to be a gradually growing, methodically operated China-Nexus ORB'; China-nexus is 'suspected', supported by Mandarin developer notes, TTPs and victimology, with no specific named state actor.
grounding: Report ties >1,000 compromised Linux SOHO/edge devices (notably Ruckus access points, Buffalo AirStation routers) into an ORB relay network via the 'ShortLeash' backdoor; devices run old/unpatched ACME mini_httpd (CVE-2015-1548, CVE-2017-17663) and DropBear SSH; victims concentrated in US, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Hong Kong across IT, networking, real-estate and media sectors. Devices serve as covert relay infrastructure, hence collateral roles.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike Intelligence assesses LIMINAL PANDA's activity 'aligns with China-nexus cyber operations' — explicitly made with LOW confidence (Pinyin strings, BRI-aligned targeting, Cobalt Strike, Vultr VPS). Tracks LIMINAL PANDA as its own actor, split from LightBasin.
summary: CrowdStrike Intelligence details LIMINAL PANDA, a suspected China-nexus actor that has targeted telecommunications providers since at least 2020 using custom tooling (CordScan, PingPong, SIGTRANslator) plus public tools (TinyShell, Fast Reverse Proxy). The actor abuses interoperational trust between telecoms and weak/default SSH credentials on externally accessible infrastructure to compromise telecom servers and pivot into other providers. Observed targets are telecom networks in Southern Asia and Africa, with tooling oriented toward GSM/SIGTRAN protocols and retrieval of subscriber data, call metadata and SMS — consistent with SIGINT objectives. CrowdStrike assesses the China-nexus alignment with low confidence based on non-exclusive indicators including Pinyin strings (e.g., wuxianpinggu507) and targeting of Belt-and-Road-aligned nations.
grounding: Blog states initial access via weak SSH authentication and abuse of telecom interconnection trust to compromise telecom servers in Southern Asia and Africa; representative target country (India) reflects the named South Asia telecom focus.

verdict_note: Microsoft describes Raspberry Typhoon as 'a nation-state activity group based out of China' targeting South China Sea interests, but the event's claimed actor is cn/mss/lotus-blossom; the source does NOT equate Raspberry Typhoon with Lotus Blossom/Billbug/Thrip. Attribution is to a China-based cluster, not the claimed Lotus Blossom.
summary: Microsoft's MSTIC (East Asia threat-landscape report) names Raspberry Typhoon, a nation-state activity group based out of China that targets entities tied to China's South China Sea interests. Microsoft reports Raspberry Typhoon successfully targeted military and executive entities in Indonesia and a Malaysian maritime system, with activity coinciding with June 2023 US-China-Indonesia naval exercises. The report does not specify the initial access vector. Microsoft does not in this report equate Raspberry Typhoon with Lotus Blossom / Billbug / Thrip.
grounding: Source ties military/executive targeting in Indonesia and a Malaysian maritime system to South China Sea-focused espionage; no entry vector disclosed.

verdict_note: Talos 'assesses with high confidence that Lotus Blossom (also referred to as Spring Dragon, Billbug, Thrip) threat actors are responsible for these campaigns', based on TTPs and Sagerunex deployment.
summary: Cisco Talos attributes, with high confidence, multiple 2025 cyber-espionage campaigns to Lotus Blossom (aka Spring Dragon, Billbug, Thrip), based on TTPs and the deployment of the Sagerunex backdoor. The campaigns target government, manufacturing, telecommunications and media sectors across Southeast Asia, including the Philippines, Vietnam, Hong Kong and Taiwan. Sagerunex variants use third-party services such as Dropbox, Twitter and Zimbra webmail for command-and-control, and operators maintain persistence via registry manipulation and service installation. Talos notes the actor has operated since at least 2012, with recent variants adding obfuscation and legitimate-cloud-service abuse for evasion. The report focuses on post-compromise activity and does not specify the initial access vector.
grounding: Talos attributes government/manufacturing/telecom/media targeting in the Philippines, Vietnam, Hong Kong and Taiwan to Lotus Blossom via Sagerunex; no initial vector stated.

verdict_note: Recorded Future Insikt Group assesses 'TAG-100 is highly likely a Chinese state-sponsored threat activity group' and now tracks it as RedNovember; matches the claimed actor cn/mss/rednovember.
summary: Recorded Future's Insikt Group tracks RedNovember (formerly TAG-100; overlaps with Microsoft's Storm-2077), assessed as highly likely a Chinese state-sponsored espionage group, across activity from June 2024 to July 2025. The group weaponizes n-day exploits against internet-facing perimeter appliances (SonicWall, Cisco ASA, F5 BIG-IP, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, Fortinet FortiGate, Ivanti Connect Secure) and uses the Go-based Pantegana backdoor, Cobalt Strike, SparkRAT and the LESLIELOADER loader, with some spearphishing. Targeting spans government, defense, aerospace, technology, law firms and intergovernmental organizations, with notable interest in US defense contractors, Taiwanese tech firms and government bodies in Panama, South Korea and Southeast Asia. Activity timing correlates with geopolitical events of strategic concern to Beijing.
grounding: RF ties scalable n-day exploitation of edge appliances plus Pantegana/Cobalt Strike to government/defense/technology targeting; representative countries (Panama, US, Taiwan, South Korea) reflect named victims.

verdict_note: Talos 'assesses with high confidence that UAT-7290 is a sophisticated threat actor falling under the China-nexus' APTs, noting significant victimology/infrastructure/tooling overlap with Recorded Future's Red Foxtrot (previously linked to PLA Unit 69010). Matches claimed actor cn/mss/uat-7290.
summary: Cisco Talos discloses UAT-7290, assessed with high confidence as a China-nexus APT active since 2022 targeting telecommunications infrastructure, primarily in South Asia with recent expansion into Southeastern Europe. Talos notes significant overlap in victimology, infrastructure and tooling with the group Recorded Future tracks as Red Foxtrot, which RF previously linked to PLA Unit 69010. The actor gains access via one-day exploits and target-specific SSH brute force against public-facing edge devices, using publicly available proof-of-concept exploit code. It deploys a Linux malware suite (RushDrop, DriveSwitch, SilentRaid, Bulbature) and builds Operational Relay Box infrastructure usable by other Chinese threat actors.
grounding: Talos ties one-day exploitation and SSH brute force of edge devices to telecom targeting in South Asia (representative country India); n-day is the dominant initial vector.

verdict_note: Talos 'assesses with high confidence that UAT-8302 is a China-nexus advanced persistent threat (APT) group' tasked with long-term access to government entities; matches claimed actor cn/mss/uat-8302. Malware overlaps noted with Jewelbug/Earth Estries/UNC5174 etc.
summary: Cisco Talos discloses UAT-8302, assessed with high confidence as a China-nexus APT group tasked primarily with obtaining and maintaining long-term access to government and related entities worldwide. The actor has targeted government entities in South America since at least late 2024 and government agencies in Southeastern Europe in 2025. It deploys multiple malware families — including NetDraft, CloudSorcerer v3 and VSHELL — that overlap with tooling used by other China-aligned clusters such as Jewelbug/REF7707, Earth Estries, Earth Naga and UNC5174. Talos says UAT-8302 follows the same paradigm of exploiting both zero-day and n-day exploits for initial access but discloses no specific entry vector for this actor; post-compromise activity centres on reconnaissance, credential extraction and lateral movement with tools like Impacket.
grounding: Source ties government-sector targeting in South America and Southeastern Europe to UAT-8302; no specific initial vector disclosed for this actor (general zero-day/n-day paradigm only), so vector left null. Representative countries (Argentina, Serbia) reflect named regions.

verdict_note: GTIG states 'UNC2814, is a suspected People's Republic of China (PRC)-nexus cyber espionage group that GTIG has tracked since 2017'; matches claimed actor cn/mss/unc2814. 'Suspected' = hedged on the PRC nexus but firmly the named cluster.
summary: Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) and Mandiant disclose GRIDTIDE, a novel C-based backdoor used by UNC2814, a suspected PRC-nexus espionage group tracked since 2017, in a global campaign against telecommunications providers and government organizations. GRIDTIDE abuses the Google Sheets API for command-and-control, disguising malicious traffic as legitimate cloud API requests. GTIG confirmed 53 victims across 42 countries on four continents (Africa, Asia and the Americas), with suspected targeting of 20+ additional countries. In February 2026 GTIG/Mandiant disrupted the operation by terminating attacker-controlled Cloud Projects and releasing IOCs. The specific initial access vector for this campaign was not determined, though UNC2814 historically exploits web servers and edge systems.
grounding: Telecom (primary) and government targeting across 42 countries; source explicitly says initial vector 'has not been determined' so vector is null. No single country named for the campaign — US shown as a representative entry only; targeting is global.

verdict_note: Mandiant 'attributed these backdoors to the China-nexus espionage group, UNC3886'; matches claimed actor cn/mss/unc3886.
summary: Mandiant attributes six variants of customized TINYSHELL backdoors deployed on Juniper Networks Junos OS routers to UNC3886, a China-nexus espionage group. The activity, discovered in mid-2024, targeted defense, technology and telecommunications organizations in the US and Asia. The actor gained privileged initial access by compromising legitimate credentials and abusing network authentication services (TACACS+) and terminal servers with access to the routers, then bypassed Junos Veriexec protection via process injection into a legitimate process (CVE-2025-21590) to run backdoors on end-of-life hardware. The backdoor variants (appid, to, irad, lmpad, jdosd, oemd) provided active and passive C2, with lmpad designed to disable logging and evade forensics during hands-on-keyboard operations.
grounding: Mandiant states initial access via compromised legitimate credentials and TACACS+/terminal-server access (hence valid-creds; the CVE-2025-21590 Veriexec bypass is a post-access technique); targets are defense, technology and telecom orgs in the US and Asia.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike tracks WARP PANDA as a China-nexus intrusion group whose operations are 'likely motivated by intelligence-collection requirements aligned with the strategic interests of the People's Republic of China (PRC)' (moderate confidence on persistence). Matches claimed actor cn/mss/warp-panda.
summary: CrowdStrike Intelligence details WARP PANDA, a China-nexus intrusion group active since 2022 whose operations are assessed as likely aligned with PRC intelligence-collection interests, targeting US legal, technology and manufacturing entities. The group gains initial access by exploiting internet-facing edge devices (such as Ivanti VPN appliances and F5 BIG-IP), then pivots into VMware vCenter environments using valid credentials or vCenter exploitation. It deploys custom malware (BRICKSTORM, Junction, GuestConduit) for persistent covert access, and uses techniques such as VM cloning and Azure session-token exfiltration to reach email and SharePoint data. One instance involved only rudimentary reconnaissance against an Asia-Pacific government entity.
grounding: CrowdStrike ties exploitation of internet-facing edge devices (Ivanti VPN, F5 BIG-IP) — an n-day vector — and subsequent vCenter/ESXi BRICKSTORM deployment to US legal/tech/manufacturing targeting; the APAC government recon is the lone collateral target (country unspecified in source).

verdict_note: Claimed actor is cn/unscoped/tgr-sta-1030 (China), but Unit 42 'assess[es] with high confidence that TGR-STA-1030 is a state-aligned group that operates out of Asia' and explicitly uses TGR-STA as a placeholder while refining attribution — it does NOT attribute to China or any specific country. The China nexus is not supported by this source.
summary: Unit 42 discloses the 'Shadow Campaigns' conducted by TGR-STA-1030 (also tracked as UNC6619), assessed with high confidence as a state-aligned group operating out of Asia — Unit 42 explicitly declines to attribute it to a specific country and uses TGR-STA as a temporary placeholder. Active since at least January 2024, the group has compromised at least 70 organizations across 37 countries (with reconnaissance against 155 countries in Nov-Dec 2025), targeting government ministries (finance, interior, foreign affairs, trade, defense), law enforcement, border control, telecommunications and critical infrastructure. Initial access is via spear-phishing (e.g., ministry-reorganization lures delivering the Diaoyu Loader from mega[.]nz) and n-day exploitation (e.g., CVE-2019-11580 Atlassian Crowd, Exchange, D-Link, SAP), with Cobalt Strike and VShell for C2. Circumstantial indicators include GMT+8 operational timing and upstream connections from AS 9808; one operator used the handle 'JackMa' (identity unconfirmed).
grounding: Unit 42 ties spear-phishing of government email recipients (plus n-day exploits) to global government-ministry targeting across 37 countries; attribution stops at 'state-aligned, operating out of Asia' with no China nexus, so the claimed cn/ actor is unsupported. No per-country breakdown provided, so target countries left null.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike explicitly tracks the vishing/extortion crews as Cordial Spider (also CL-CRI-1116 / UNC6671) and Snarky Spider, closely aligned with Scattered Spider and The Com.
summary: CrowdStrike detailed two financially motivated e-crime crews it tracks as Cordial Spider (also CL-CRI-1116 / UNC6671) and Snarky Spider, part of a new wave of data-theft-and-extortion actors closely aligned with Scattered Spider and other subsets of The Com (including SLSH and ShinyHunters). The groups use voice-phishing (vishing), text messages and emails to direct targeted employees to phishing pages spoofing their employer's legitimate single sign-on portal, capturing credentials, session keys and tokens to break into identity and SaaS platforms. CrowdStrike says they primarily target U.S.-based organizations across the academic, aviation, retail, hospitality, automotive, financial-services, legal and technology sectors. Cordial Spider is associated with a data-leak site referred to as BlackFile.
grounding: Article maps the vishing-to-fake-SSO initial access and the named U.S. sector list (academic/aviation/retail/hospitality/automotive/financial/legal/technology) directly to CrowdStrike's Cordial Spider and Snarky Spider tracking.

verdict_note: Google TAG states the iOS exploit chain was developed by commercial surveillance vendor Intellexa to install its Predator spyware.
summary: Google's Threat Analysis Group (TAG), with The Citizen Lab, disclosed an in-the-wild iOS zero-day exploit chain developed by the commercial surveillance vendor Intellexa to covertly install its Predator spyware. Delivery used man-in-the-middle network injection via HTTP interception (and, on Android, one-time links sent directly to the target). The reporting ties the operation to the targeting of Ahmed Eltantawy, a former Egyptian member of parliament and opposition figure, in Egypt. TAG attributes the exploit chain and Predator spyware to Intellexa.
grounding: TAG explicitly names Intellexa/Predator and the iOS 0-day chain; the Citizen Lab victim (Eltantawy, an Egyptian opposition politician) grounds the civil-society/dissident target in Egypt.

verdict_note: Article (citing the joint CISA/FBI/HHS/MS-ISAC #StopRansomware advisory) attributes the activity to the Interlock ransomware-as-a-service operation.
grounding: Article ties Interlock's drive-by fake-installer downloads and ClickFix social engineering to attacks on healthcare/HPH and critical-infrastructure orgs across North America and Europe (named victims include DaVita, Kettering Health, Texas Tech University Health Sciences Center).

verdict_note: Wikipedia states a group calling itself 'Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters' claimed responsibility on Telegram, indicating collaboration of Scattered Spider, Lapsus$ and ShinyHunters.
grounding: Source attributes the JLR (UK automotive manufacturer) production-halting attack to a self-claimed 'Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters' Telegram claim; it gives no initial-access detail, so vector is left null.

verdict_note: BleepingComputer states the actors claim to be part of ShinyHunters, Scattered Spider and Lapsus$, collectively self-branded 'Scattered Lapsus$ Hunters'.
grounding: Article documents voice-phishing plus malicious OAuth-app and Salesloft Drift token abuse against Salesforce customers; the 39 named leak-site victims (FedEx/UPS, Disney/HBO, Home Depot/IKEA, Google/Cisco, Toyota, Marriott, Air France-KLM, TransUnion) ground the multi-sector US/global targets and the shinyhunters-salesforce-2025 campaign.

verdict_note: Microsoft (MSTIC) attributes the multi-staged hybrid-cloud ransomware activity to the actor it tracks as Storm-0501.
summary: Microsoft Threat Intelligence (MSTIC) detailed a multi-staged campaign in which the financially motivated actor it tracks as Storm-0501 extended on-premises intrusions into victims' Azure and Entra ID (hybrid cloud) environments to steal data, create persistent backdoor access and deploy ransomware. Initial access was gained via stolen/compromised credentials and by exploiting known remote-code-execution vulnerabilities in unpatched public-facing servers, including CVE-2022-47966 (Zoho ManageEngine), CVE-2023-4966 (Citrix NetScaler) and a ColdFusion 2016 flaw. MSTIC says Storm-0501 has targeted multiple U.S. sectors including government, manufacturing, transportation, law enforcement and hospitals. The group has deployed several ransomware families over time (Hive, BlackCat/ALPHV, Hunters International, LockBit and, most recently, Embargo).
grounding: MSTIC's named n-day RCE exploitation (CVE-2022-47966 / CVE-2023-4966 / ColdFusion) and stolen-credential access plus the stated U.S. sector list (government/manufacturing/transportation/law-enforcement/hospitals) ground the vector and targets for Storm-0501.

verdict_note: War on the Rocks states France's information-manipulation agency (VIGINUM) assessed Storm-1516 as operating from Russia with ties to the Kremlin, alongside the Foreign Ministry condemning Russia's destabilizing activities.
summary: War on the Rocks describes France's shift toward public attribution, including a May 2025 VIGINUM assessment of the Storm-1516 information-manipulation operation that characterized the group as operating from Russia with ties to the Kremlin. The disclosure was paired with the French Ministry for Europe and Foreign Affairs condemning Russia's 'destabilizing activities.' The article situates this as part of France hardening its stance on naming Russian influence and disinformation activity. It does not enumerate Storm-1516's specific targets.
grounding: Source confirms the French government (VIGINUM/SGDSN + Foreign Ministry) publicly tied the Storm-1516 information-manipulation campaign to Russia; it provides no target sectors, so targets are left empty and vector null.

verdict_note: CFR lists Israel only as 'Suspected state sponsor' (hedged, not confirmed) for the Duqu 2.0 espionage operation.
grounding: CFR (suspected sponsor: Israel) ties Duqu 2.0 to espionage against individuals/companies linked to P5+1 Iran nuclear talks, including hotels hosting the negotiations and Kaspersky Lab; hospitality (talks venues) and ICT/security (Kaspersky) targets follow, with venue locations such as Austria.

verdict_note: CFR states the threat actor was 'believed to be the Israeli government' — a hedged/suspected attribution, not confirmed.
grounding: CFR attributes (suspected) to the Israeli government the compromise of Russia-based cybersecurity firm Kaspersky Lab (ICT/security, Russia), which let Israel observe Russian searches of Kaspersky telemetry for NSA tooling.

verdict_note: CFR lists Israel as 'Suspected state sponsor' (with later Israeli-media confirmation noted) for the disruptive attack on Shahid Rajaee port.
grounding: CFR (suspected sponsor: Israel) records a disruptive/sabotage cyberattack on Iran's Shahid Rajaee port, grounding the transport/maritime target in Iran; no initial-access method is described.

verdict_note: CFR lists the activity as 'believed to be the work of Israel's government' / suspected — hedged, not confirmed, regarding the Israeli police sigint unit.
grounding: CFR (suspected: Israel's government) records warrantless use of NSO Pegasus to surveil mayors/local officials and their families in Israel, grounding the government/local target in Israel; no technical vector is detailed.

verdict_note: EclecticIQ attributes the spear-phishing to Bitter APT (TA397) but only 'very likely' and calls it a 'South Asian state-sponsored actor' — it does NOT confirm the India/NTRO nexus asserted by the actor_id.
grounding: EclecticIQ ties a spear-phishing chain (IQY/Excel-macro delivering a WmRAT variant, using credentials stolen from Pakistan's Counter Terrorism Department via StealC) to Bitter/TA397 targeting PTCL telecom staff in Pakistan; vector=phishing, target=ict/telecom/carrier in PK. India-nexus is hedged in this source.

verdict_note: Proofpoint assesses 'it is highly likely that TA397 [Bitter] is a state-backed threat actor tasked with intelligence gathering in the interests of the Indian state' (IST working hours, exfiltrated Bangladeshi gov docs).
grounding: Source ties spearphishing (RAR/CHM/LNK/MSC attachments; CVE-2024-43572) against government, diplomatic and defense entities tied to China, Pakistan and the Indian subcontinent, plus recovered Bangladeshi government documents, to the India-aligned TA397/Bitter.

verdict_note: Fortinet names this activity as Confucius (the claimed actor) but characterizes it only as state-sponsored South Asian activity 'believed to have links to state-sponsored operations in the region'; it does NOT confirm the India/pro-India nexus the actor_id encodes.
grounding: Source ties spear-phishing (PPSX, malicious LNK, DLL side-loading via fixmapi.exe) delivering WooperStealer then the Python AnonDoor backdoor against Pakistani government, military and critical-infrastructure targets to Confucius across Dec 2024-Sep 2025.

verdict_note: Kaspersky GReAT names the campaign as Mysterious Elephant (APT-K-47), the claimed actor, but provides NO state attribution at all — India is not named, so the IN nexus on the actor_id is unsupported by this source (consistent with the actor_id's 'unscoped' marker).
grounding: Source ties personalized spear-phishing and malicious documents (BabShell reverse shell, MemLoader HidenDesk, PowerShell) stealing documents/images/archives and WhatsApp data from government and foreign-affairs orgs in Pakistan, Bangladesh, Afghanistan, Nepal and Sri Lanka to Mysterious Elephant/APT-K-47.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike attributes the activity to IMPERIAL KITTEN (the claimed actor), 'an Iran-nexus adversary with a suspected connection to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC)' (moderate confidence overall); the IRGC link is 'suspected' rather than confirmed. The report does not restate the Tortoiseshell/TA456 aliases.
grounding: Source ties an Oct 2023 wave against Israeli transportation, maritime, logistics, technology and defense orgs to IMPERIAL KITTEN; access mixed strategic web compromise (watering-hole — no clean vocab enum, hence initial_vector null), one-day exploits, stolen VPN credentials, SQLi and phishing, delivering the IMAPLoader implant.

verdict_note: Unit 42 assesses 'with a high level of confidence' the attacks were carried out by 'the Iranian-linked Agonizing Serpens APT group (aka Agrius)' — explicitly the claimed actor and alias. Note: source says 'Iranian-linked/Iranian-backed', not MOIS specifically (actor_id encodes MOIS).
grounding: Source ties initial access by 'exploiting vulnerable internet facing web servers' then ASPXSpy web shells, PII/IP theft and the novel MultiLayer/PartialWasher/BFG Agonizer wipers against Israeli higher-education and technology orgs (Jan-Oct 2023) to Agrius/Agonizing Serpens.

verdict_note: ESET describes BladedFeline (the claimed actor) as 'an Iran-aligned APT group' and assesses 'with medium confidence that BladedFeline is a subgroup of OilRig' (shared targeting, OilRig VideoSRV/RDAT tools on KRG systems, PrimeCache/RDAT code overlap). Source says 'Iran-aligned', not MOIS explicitly (actor_id encodes MOIS).
summary: ESET Research details BladedFeline, an Iran-aligned cyberespionage group assessed with medium confidence to be a subgroup of OilRig (APT34), that has maintained persistent access to Kurdistan Regional Government diplomatic officials since at least 2017 and to high-ranking Iraqi government officials, as well as a regional telecommunications provider in Uzbekistan. The group runs a custom toolkit including the Whisper and Shahmaran backdoors, the PrimeCache malicious IIS module, the Flog webshell and Laret/Pinar reverse tunnels. For the Iraqi government victims ESET suspects the operators exploited a vulnerability in an internet-facing web server to deploy the Flog webshell, though the access vector for the earlier KRG compromises remains unclear. The OilRig linkage rests on shared Middle East targeting, recovery of OilRig's VideoSRV and RDAT tools on compromised KRG systems, and code similarities between PrimeCache and OilRig's RDAT backdoor. Activity tracked runs from September 2017 through tool uploads in March 2024.
grounding: Source ties Whisper/Shahmaran/PrimeCache espionage tooling and a suspected internet-facing web-server exploit (Flog webshell) against Kurdish and Iraqi government officials (and an Uzbek telecom) to the OilRig subgroup BladedFeline.

verdict_note: MISMAP: actor_id is ir/mois/handala, but the source is entirely about Void Manticore (aka Storm-842), 'an Iranian threat actor affiliated with MOIS' operating the 'Karma' persona — it never mentions Handala. The op is attributed to Void Manticore/Karma, NOT to the claimed actor Handala.
grounding: Source attributes destructive operations against 40+ Israeli organizations (CVE-2019-0604 web-server exploitation + Karma Shell webshells; BiBi/Cl/partition wipers) to Void Manticore, which receives long-dwell access handed off from Scarred Manticore — no sectors specified beyond 'high-value', and Handala is never named, so the claimed actor is unsupported.

verdict_note: Check Point/Sygnia attribute the campaign to Scarred Manticore (the claimed actor), 'an actor affiliated with the Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS)', most closely aligned with DEV-0861 and likely related to OilRig/APT34 (behind the 2021 Albania attack).
grounding: Source ties a long-dwell espionage campaign exploiting internet-facing Windows/SharePoint/IIS servers and the custom LIONTAIL passive implant (abusing undocumented HTTP.sys IOCTLs) against government, military, telecom, financial, IT-services and NGO targets across Saudi Arabia, UAE, Jordan, Kuwait, Oman, Iraq, Israel and Albania to the MOIS-affiliated Scarred Manticore.

verdict_note: GTIG attributes the axios compromise to UNC1069, 'a financially motivated North Korea-nexus threat actor' (based on WAVESHAPER.V2 reuse + infrastructure overlap). The source does NOT equate UNC1069 with BlueNoroff/Sapphire Sleet/Stardust Chollima — the actor_id's BlueNoroff alias is a corpus mapping, not stated here.
grounding: Source ties a supply-chain compromise of the axios npm package (maintainer account takeover; malicious axios 1.14.1/0.30.4 pulling the plain-crypto-js dependency / SILKBELL dropper delivering OS-specific WAVESHAPER.V2 backdoors) on 31 Mar 2026 to the financially motivated NK actor UNC1069; downstream impact is the broad developer/software supply chain.

verdict_note: CrowdStrike attributes this to GOLDEN CHOLLIMA, a DPRK-nexus adversary (one of three subgroups evolved from LABYRINTH CHOLLIMA). The source uses only CrowdStrike CHOLLIMA naming and does NOT use the 'Citrine Sleet' alias the actor_id encodes, so the GOLDEN CHOLLIMA = Citrine Sleet equivalence is a corpus mapping, not stated here.
summary: CrowdStrike Intelligence describes GOLDEN CHOLLIMA as a DPRK-nexus adversary, one of three distinct subgroups that evolved from LABYRINTH CHOLLIMA since 2018 and that focuses on consistent cryptocurrency theft to generate revenue for the regime. In late 2024 the group delivered malicious Python packages via recruitment fraud to a European fintech company, then pivoted into the victim's cloud environment to access IAM configurations and cloud resources, ultimately diverting the victim's cryptocurrency to adversary-controlled wallets. CrowdStrike notes GOLDEN CHOLLIMA targets economically developed regions with significant fintech presence, including the U.S., Canada, South Korea, India and Western Europe. The subgroup wields a specialized malware toolkit and shares certain tools (e.g., FudModule) and infrastructure with other CHOLLIMA units, indicating centralized DPRK coordination. The report does not name individual operators or a specific victim-country for the European fintech intrusion.
grounding: Source ties a late-2024 recruitment-fraud lure (malicious Python packages), a cloud pivot to IAM/cloud resources, and cryptocurrency theft against a European fintech to the DPRK-nexus GOLDEN CHOLLIMA.

verdict_note: MSTIC describes Ruby Sleet as a North Korea-nexus actor it has tracked since 2020, presented as a distinct entity (separate from Sapphire Sleet). The source does NOT relate Ruby Sleet to Kimsuky, so the actor_id's Kimsuky mapping is not supported by this source.
summary: At CYBERWARCON 2024, Microsoft (MSTIC) detailed Ruby Sleet, a North Korea-nexus threat actor it has tracked since 2020 that targets and has successfully compromised aerospace and defense-related organizations, including satellite and weapons-systems-related targets, primarily through increasingly sophisticated phishing. Microsoft observed Ruby Sleet conduct a supply-chain compromise in December 2023 by breaching a Korean construction company and replacing a legitimate version of the VeraPort software-management client with a backdoored version that communicated with Ruby Sleet infrastructure. The group also leveraged compromised legitimate code-signing certificates, backdoored VPN clients and modified software installers to deliver its implants. Microsoft presented Ruby Sleet as a distinct tracked actor within the North Korean threat landscape, separate from Sapphire Sleet. The Ruby Sleet section names no specific malware families or individual operators.
grounding: Source ties a December 2023 VeraPort supply-chain compromise (via a breached Korean construction company, used as a staging vector) plus sophisticated phishing against aerospace, satellite and defense targets to the NK actor Ruby Sleet.

verdict_note: CSIS confirms the Republic of Korea (Office of the President) released its National Cybersecurity Strategy in February 2024 — this is a doctrine/policy publication by the claimed actor (ROK National Security Office), not an adversary intrusion, so attribution is to the publishing state itself.
grounding: Source confirms a doctrine milestone: South Korea's Feb-2024 National Cybersecurity Strategy explicitly transitions from the 2019 defensive posture to a proactive/offensive paradigm ('we must change our paradigm to respond offensively to North Korea and other threats'), naming North Korea as the primary threat and mirroring the US 'defend forward' concept — not a cyber-intrusion event, so no initial vector or targets.

verdict_note: CFR names the actor as 'DarkHotel, a South Korea-linked threat actor'; lists Korea (Republic of) as suspected sponsor.
grounding: CFR: DarkHotel burned five zero-days in a single campaign against North Koreans and North-Korea-focused professionals (government sector).

verdict_note: CFR attributes to APT-C-23 (Hamas-linked; Arid Viper alias), suspected sponsor 'Palestine, State of'.
grounding: CFR: fabricated Facebook personas lured Israeli defense, law-enforcement and emergency-services officials into installing trojanized messaging apps (social-engineering delivery).

verdict_note: CFR names Arid Viper (a.k.a. APT-C-23) 'believed to operate from the Palestinian Territories'; suspected sponsor 'Palestine, State of'.
grounding: CFR: Arid Viper built a trojanized clone of the Arabic dating app 'Skipped' distributed online to deliver Android spyware to civil-society users (social-engineering app lure).

verdict_note: CFR attributes to Molerats, 'believed to be the work of Palestine's government'; suspected sponsor 'Palestine, State of'.
grounding: CFR: Molerats spear-phished Middle Eastern governments, foreign-policy think tanks and a state-affiliated airline, delivering a geofenced RAT via Quora/Dropbox/WordPress lures; region-only, no single country named.

verdict_note: DOJ unsealed a 16-defendant indictment against the Russia-based DanaBot operators (two named); CrowdStrike ('Scully Spider') assesses they acted on behalf of Russian government interests.
grounding: CyberScoop/DOJ: a second DanaBot variant collected from military, diplomatic and law-enforcement personnel across North America and Europe to a separate server; espionage nexus is CrowdStrike's assessment; article does not state the initial infection vector (so unknown); regions only, no single country.

verdict_note: GTIG: 'UNC4221 (tracked by CERT-UA as UAC-0185) is an additional Russia-linked threat actor' targeting Ukrainian military Signal accounts.
summary: Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG) details UNC4221 (CERT-UA: UAC-0185), a Russia-linked threat actor that targets Signal accounts used by Ukrainian military personnel. The group operates a tailored Signal phishing kit that mimics components of Kropyva, the artillery-guidance application used by the Armed Forces of Ukraine, presenting malicious device-linking QR codes that link victims' Signal accounts to actor-controlled devices. As a core component, it also deploys a lightweight JavaScript payload tracked as PINPOINT to collect basic user information and geolocation via the browser's GeoLocation API. Earlier 2022 operations used phishing pages crafted as Signal security alerts. The technique grants persistent access to sensitive communications without full device compromise.
grounding: GTIG: device-linking QR-code phishing pages mimicking Signal/Kropyva targeted Ukrainian armed-forces (defense) personnel in Ukraine.

verdict_note: Volexity 'attributes these new campaigns to the Russian threat actor it tracks as UTA0355' (same actor as its April 2025 M365 OAuth reporting).
grounding: Volexity: UTA0355 spoofed real European security events (Belgrade Security Conference, Brussels Indo-Pacific Dialogue, World Nuclear Exhibition) in OAuth and device-code phishing of foreign-policy experts and former senior US officials.

verdict_note: CFR records a Saudi-linked operator using NSO Pegasus against Abdulaziz; suspected sponsor Saudi Arabia; cites the Citizen Lab 'KINGDOM came to Canada' report (source of the KINGDOM operator label).
grounding: CFR: a threat actor used NSO Pegasus against the iPhone of Omar Abdulaziz, a Saudi critic living in Canada (civil-society/dissident); CFR page does not state the delivery method, so initial_vector left null.
